content:
# Final Review Proposal
**Producer:** agent:codex/gpt-5.4-xhigh

## Summary

Reviewed the branch against `origin/master`, including the prompt-generation path, backend/schema changes, and targeted tests. I found two substantive issues: prompt output is hash-unstable when `br list --json` transiently fails, and planned-elsewhere extraction ignores milestone acceptance ownership so related cross-workstream beads can be omitted.

## Amendments

1. Do not silently degrade prompt generation when `br list --json` fails. `handle_create_from_bead` currently converts any `AppError::Io` from `load_bead_summaries` into an empty summary map (`src/cli/project.rs:296-300`), and the downstream renderers then emit fallback/`unknown` dependency data when those summaries are missing (`src/cli/project.rs:1154-1167`, `src/cli/project.rs:1358-1366`). That means the same milestone/bead state can produce different `prompt.md` contents and different prompt hashes depending on transient `br list` availability, which violates the bead’s deterministic-rendering requirement. Either fail hard on generic `br list` failures or source the needed status/title fields from deterministic project data instead of silently dropping them.
   Rationale: This directly breaks the acceptance criterion that prompt rendering be stable for hash-based drift detection.
2. Planned-elsewhere generation should incorporate the milestone plan’s explicit acceptance ownership, not just dependents and adjacent same-workstream beads. `build_planned_elsewhere_context` only considers `bead.dependents`, `bead.dependencies`, and +/-1 workstream neighbors (`src/cli/project.rs:1253-1376`), even though the bundle already carries authoritative `acceptance_map[].covered_by` ownership data for which beads own related milestone criteria (`src/contexts/milestone_record/bundle.rs:51-52`, `src/contexts/milestone_record/bundle.rs:704-713`). As written, a related bead in another workstream with no direct dependency edge will be omitted from `## Already Planned Elsewhere`, so the prompt can miss the very planned follow-up work that is supposed to prevent scope creep and over-demanding reviews.
   Rationale: This leaves out authoritative planned-elsewhere context in common cross-workstream cases, reducing prompt self-containment and scope clarity.
